speaker: interviewer
text: It's June 5th, 1996. This interview is with Lillian Saunders. Lillian, we-- you had just-- you were still in the tuberculosis sanatorium in Prague. Where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were given a chance to go inland in Czechoslovakia. Excuse me. And we got a house from the Germans who fled and a house and a job. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we worked in that factory, and my brother was still in a sanatorium. And we had-- my friend, Alice, from home has left her mother in Russia and came to us. And we stayed. The three girls-- we were together, and we tried to do housekeeping and try a normal life, which was very difficult. And then one day, the Zionist organization sent over people to tell us that the border will be closed down, and we won't be able to leave Czechoslovakia, to get our stuff together and go. We were taken to As at the Czech border, and we crossed into Germany, and we were taken to Munich. There were those little things happening on the road where the Czech soldiers were paid off to let us out. And they were trying to take advantage, but they were pretty decent. And then we got to Germany, and there we were in a-- a-- in a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: A DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a DP camp to wait-- to wait for emigration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Munich in Gabarsee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gabarsee. Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-A-B-A-R-S-E-E. And we were trying-- we were looking for relatives where we should go. And we knew my uncle, Adolf Schoenberger. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we have written to the organizations through the Joint, the UNRRA, and we got a reply and a letter from my uncle that yes, he is going to take us out. And he is going to send papers. And his daughter that tells us now that he wanted to adopt us, and then we get a letter that he died. And then my Uncle Isidore took the paper. He took over the case, and he applied for us to come out. And his boss, Mr. Bass, B-A-S-S, he-- he guaranteed for us. My uncle worked for him--

speaker: interviewer
text: To come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: To come to the United States. My uncle worked for him, and my uncle's integrity was enough of a guarantee for him that we will not be a charge to him. And he signed, and we came out to my uncle. And my uncle-- then he gave jobs to my sister and to my brother, Gene. Mr. Bass gave jobs. And I went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and my uncle bought a house in the Bronx, and he went to this builder, and he says this is my family. What do you have? And he says, I have this house. How much is it? 18.5. He says, here is $500.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the man says, Mr. Schoenberger, I buy shoes with greater difficulty. And he says, the girls need a home. You have a house. Here is the money. And he bought it. Then we started to gather things. We went into the store. We bought-- he bought furniture, and he went to the hardware store, and he says, Benny, these girls are going to keep house. Give them pots and pans. And we had started a life here. And then we were hoping somehow to get my brother Moric out from Russia, but that didn't happen for many years. But eventually it happened. And he came out with his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you met your husband at some point?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, there was a couple who had a child. That was very unusual, because everyone came from the concentration camps, and they came to this camp to emigrate to America, but they were not in concentration camp. And one day I was going to school in Munich, and on the way home I was always walking from the train station. And there zipped by me a motorcycle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And further up, about a mile away, I see-- the long expanses, you could see far away. And I see he stopped. And I walk, and then when I get towards-- get to this man, I said, can I help you? Because he looked like he has a-- has a problem. He says, there's a cinder in my eyes. So I say, you have a handkerchief? And I took his handkerchief, made a corner, lifted up his eyelid, and I removed the cinder. He was very grateful, and he happened to be a physician. And he says, would you like a ride to the camp? I said, thank you. I sat on the motorcycle, and I went to camp with him into Gabarsee. He says, my wife and my son live here. Why don't you visit us? And I did. And I fell in love with that little child. That was the first child after the war. And we were friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they came to America, and I came with my sister and my brother to America, they invited me for dinner. And when I get there, there is this man. And that's how I met my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the man's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leslie Saunders. But his name was Schwartz before the war, and [NON-ENGLISH] after the war, and then Saunders in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met in '50, end of '50, and we were married in '52.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have two sons-- Richard who is 41, and Gary who is 39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they married?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Richard, my older, has two sons-- two geniuses. And my son Gary, whose son is five months, a definite genius. And I'm very, very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What have you done all this time since you got to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides raising a family.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came, I went to school, and then I went to become a bookkeeper. And I worked as a bookkeeper in a [? turkey ?] restaurant, in a vest, coat and apron. And then when I was expecting Richard, they gave me a big party, and they gave me linens and-- and gifts, and they packed me off, and I had Richard soon. And then I became a very devoted mother and all the things that children are supposed to get from loving parents, and I did that. Then when they got a little bigger, I went into the business to help in the store, in our furniture store. We had a furniture store, and I helped there. And from the furniture, I-- I said, you know, I really would like to do some arranging of furniture. So I took a few courses in Parsons. And then whoever needed interior designing, decorating, I did it. So now I do-- somehow, I don't know how it came about-- I tear out bathrooms, and I do bathrooms. And it comes out very nicely. And-- and my children have my services, my nieces and whoever wants services, and friends. And so I do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of all that tragedy, you rebuilt a life.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you don't go a little ahead, you fall back. Well, I have not done what I should have done, but that is the should have I won't go into. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll meet your husband shortly. Was there any kind of message that you would want to convey to people who might view this tape someday in the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just realize that you have to be logical and-- and care and not to be too selfish. But also be logical, and give to others and be kind, which is very-- this is not-- it's not easy, because humanity has many sides. And not many sides are good. And we don't have much love in our hearts, evidently, seeing what the world is like. But in individual lives, there should be kindness, and give and care, because the big picture is very bleak. And unless we can make our little corner good and pleasant, we cannot make more than that, because the world is-- resists. On a big picture, everything is resisted. On a small scale, we have to make our home and our little world good, our little section good. And I hope I have done that. My children think I was a pushover, and I spoiled them. So now they spoil their kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: They learned from their mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Leslie. I call him [NON-ENGLISH] Saunders. And we're married for 43 years. And we have two sons. And what do you have to add to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot ADD any more children. Two sons-- that's what we have. And well, I suffered with Lillie [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after when I heard the things she went through. And I lived [INAUDIBLE] too. And it is a nightmare even to hear it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You were in the labor camp, and you were hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was in-- in labor camp in Hungary, but, naturally, my experiences were nothing close to what she went through. My experiences were mostly fear, fear of being eliminated, fear of fighting the unknown what comes to us. But as far as personal knowledge of any of the German camps, I had none. I take it that you have succeeded in-- that you've succeeded. You and Lillian have succeeded in overcoming the nightmare of those terrible years.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That you have succeeded in overcoming the nightmares of my years in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of your years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually you know, she-- she carried that well. She was trying to live for today. But here and there, the memories of yesterday came through in remarks, in behavior, in fear for her children. And all that fear which was implanted in her sometimes and another have come across and made her present and past life together, or present-- sometimes very difficult for her because of the memories. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All and all, I think the damage done to people who were in concentration camps is not only-- was during the time of the concentration camp, but they have to carry this terrible burden with them all through their life, never to be forgotten, and sometimes more vivid than it was when they were there, because when you are there, there's nothing you can do about it. You just try to stay alive from day to day. But after, the memories are devastating. And this I-- this I have seen in my wife. And this not only affects the person who lived it through, and-- but the children also. My sons, even though [INAUDIBLE] they outwardly don't acknowledge it, and their mother was trying to shield them from her memories, all the way through. As a matter of fact, her memories were so bad that for very many years, she wasn't able to speak about it, to mention it whatsoever. Maybe it was 20 or 25 or 30 years after we got married that it started to-- she started to be able to talk about it and describe what happened to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose picture is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is grandma Esther, my father's mother. She lived to be 93 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Budapest. She lived in Budapest when this was taken. But she moved to Munkacs to be near us, and that's where she died in 1938 or '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea how old she might have been at the time this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I always wondered. She could have been 55 or 60, or the clothes may have makes her look older. But she lived to be 93. Her face looks like it may be 50-- 50, 60 year old. But I can't be sure. This is my in the middle, is-- seated, is my father Nathan Koenigsbach, his brother Adolf Schoenberger, and the sister is either [NON-ENGLISH] or Paula. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately when that was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It could have been taken in the early 1900s. Early 1900s. This was taken in 1921. My father was home from the war a little over a year. And from the left is my brother, Adolf, my mother, my sister Regi, my father, and my sister, Helen. This is my brother Adolf in Munkacs in the park and taken in the late 1930s. This is my sister, Helen. She made her-- her costume. And she-- this was a New Year's Eve ball, and she won first beauty prize. I remember this was a very special occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall approximately what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Late '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did these photographs survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some relatives in America had these pictures. We had none at all. We couldn't find anything. This is my sister, Regi. She was dancing in her school and got 11 curtain calls. She was very good at it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She must have been either 15, 14, 15, or 16, and she was born in 1920. This is my brother, Moric. And this must have been taken in early 1940. And he was the one, I said, with the music. He was always playing the accordion and was very good leather worker. This is my brother Jeno, and he was in a TB hospital in 1946. There's one of his outings.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is I, at the age of one year. Your name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lillian Koenigsbach Saunders. This was taken in the Bernsteins' back-- in the-- where we lived, in Bernsteins' backyard. And I remember holding my sister's hand very high up and going on the cobblestones, which was very, very hard on me. And then when I entered the studio, I cried. And they handed me a toy, which is there. That's a Teddy bear, and I smiled after that. And no one wants to believe me that I have that memory, but I do. This is my sister Helen's wedding in 1936 or '37. And from left is my brother, Moric.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which row?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first row standing is Moric, Adolf, my sister Helen, her fiance Moritz, sister Regi, and the fiance's brother. And from-- again from the left, my aunt [? Teri ?], the father's-- the groom's mother, my father, my mother, and Jeno, my brother Jeno. Again, sitting on the floor is my cousin, Alex-- the little boy who didn't come back from concentration camp-- my cousin Betty, and I. I gave the names. This is my uncle Isidor Schoenberger and I, and we are sitting on the steps on the house in the Bronx he bought when we came out in 1950. 1949 in July we came to this country. This is my husband Leslie and I on somebody's wedding. In-- It may have been in 1960 or '65. This is my son Richard, my first born, and his wife Eileen. Her maiden name is Kramer. And my grandson Matthew, who is 11 years old, and my younger grandson Stephen, who is 6, who was 6 on June 2nd. And they live in Pennsylvania, and they are very wonderful people. This was taken in-- in May 1960-- 1996. This is my son, Gary, and his wife, Adele. Her maiden name is Zola, and they got married June 12, two years ago, and they are wonderful children. And they live in Manhattan right now. This is William Ryan Saunders, and he is now five months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose child is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Gary's and Adele's son. My younger son's son.
